definition: The words 'a' and 'an' in English. You use them before a noun when you talk about something for the first time or something that is not specific.
exampleSentence: I have a book.
contextPassage: In English class, we learn to use 'a' before consonant sounds and 'an' before vowel sounds. For example, we say 'a cat' but 'an apple'.

definition: In a higher place than something else; over something.
exampleSentence: The bridge is above the river.
contextPassage: In engineering, 'above' helps us describe where things are. For example, a pipe can be above a wall or a wire can go above the ground.

definition: Something you do. A thing a person does on purpose.
exampleSentence: The lawyer looked at every action the man did that day.
contextPassage: In law, an action is something a person does that can be right or wrong. In sports, an action is a move a player makes during a game.

definition: Something you do, often to stay healthy or to learn. Being active and moving.
exampleSentence: Running is a good activity for your health.
contextPassage: Doctors say that physical activity is very good for your body. In school, students do fun activities to help them learn new things.

definition: To put one thing with another thing to make more. To put numbers together.
exampleSentence: Add two and three to get five.
contextPassage: In math class, we learn to add numbers together. In engineering, people add parts to build things like bridges and roads.

definition: The name of the place where someone lives or works. It tells you where something is, like a street and a number.
exampleSentence: Write your address on the paper so we can send you a letter.
contextPassage: In law, your address tells people where you live. It is very important in legal papers so people can find and contact you.

definition: A person who is fully grown up. A person who is not a child anymore.
exampleSentence: You must be an adult to sign this paper.
contextPassage: In law, an adult is a person who is old enough to make their own decisions. In health, adults need to eat well and exercise to stay strong.

definition: Words that help you know what to do. When someone tells you what they think is good for you.
exampleSentence: The doctor gave me good advice about eating healthy food.
contextPassage: A doctor gives you health advice to help you feel better and stay well. A lawyer gives you legal advice to help you understand your rights.

definition: How old someone or something is. The number of years a person has lived.
exampleSentence: You need to know a player's age before they can join the team.
contextPassage: In sports, age can decide which team a player joins. In law and health, age is important because some rules and care are different for young people and old people.

definition: In the past. Before now.
exampleSentence: The man signed the paper two years ago.
contextPassage: In law, it is important to know when something happened. We use 'ago' to say how far back in the past something took place, like 'five days ago'.

definition: To say yes to something. To think the same as another person.
exampleSentence: Both sides agree to follow the new rules.
contextPassage: In law, when two people agree, they say yes to the same thing, and this can make a contract. It is very important that both sides agree before they sign any paper.

definition: The invisible thing all around us that we breathe. It has no color and no smell.
exampleSentence: The engineer checks the air quality inside the building.
contextPassage: We need clean air to breathe and stay healthy. Engineers work to make sure the air inside buildings is clean and safe for people.

definition: A living thing that can move and eat. It is not a plant. Dogs, cats, and birds are animals.
exampleSentence: The children learned about each animal in class today.
contextPassage: In school, we learn about many different animals and where they live. Eating animals or animal products can give our bodies important things we need to stay healthy.

definition: What you say or write when someone asks you a question. A reply to a question.
exampleSentence: Please write your answer on the paper.
contextPassage: In school, a teacher asks a question and a student gives an answer. A good answer shows that you understand what you learned.

definition: The size of a flat surface. We measure area to know how big a space is.
exampleSentence: The area of the sports field is very big.
contextPassage: In engineering, we measure the area of land or buildings to plan and build things. In sports, each part of a field has its own area with special rules.

definition: The part of your body from your shoulder to your hand.
exampleSentence: The doctor looked at my arm after I fell.
contextPassage: Your arm is very important for moving and doing daily tasks. In sports, a strong arm helps you throw and catch a ball.

definition: A long weapon with a sharp blade on a stick, used by soldiers long ago.
exampleSentence: The soldier carried a bill in the old war.
contextPassage: A bill was a weapon that soldiers used many hundreds of years ago. It had a sharp, heavy blade at the top of a long pole.

definition: A website where a person writes about their ideas and life, like an online diary.
exampleSentence: The teacher writes a blog to help students learn new words.
contextPassage: A blog is a place on the internet where people share their thoughts and ideas. Students and teachers can use blogs to learn and talk about many topics.

definition: All the parts of a person, like the head, arms, and legs together.
exampleSentence: You must take care of your body to stay healthy.
contextPassage: Your body is everything that makes up you on the outside. In sports and health, keeping your body strong and healthy is very important.

definition: Many pages put together so you can read words or see pictures.
exampleSentence: The student reads a book every day at school.
contextPassage: A book has many pages with words and pictures inside. We use books at school to learn new things every day.

definition: A strong, big shoe that covers your foot and part of your leg.
exampleSentence: He wears boots when he plays football.
contextPassage: A boot is a type of shoe that is big and strong. In many sports, players wear boots to protect their feet and legs.

definition: To come into the world for the first time, like a baby.
exampleSentence: The baby was born at the hospital.
contextPassage: When a baby is born, it comes into the world for the very first time. Doctors help to make sure the baby and mother are safe and healthy.

definition: A square or rectangle shape with four sides that can hold things inside.
exampleSentence: The player stepped into the box to kick the ball.
contextPassage: In sports like football, a box is a square area on the ground with clear lines. Important parts of the game happen inside this box.

definition: A young male child.
exampleSentence: The boy reads his book in class.
contextPassage: A boy is a young male who is still growing up. Boys go to school to learn and play with their friends every day.

definition: A soft food made from flour and water that is cooked in an oven.
exampleSentence: She eats bread every morning to feel strong.
contextPassage: Bread is a very common food that many people eat every day. It gives your body energy to move and work.

definition: When something is split into two or more pieces.
exampleSentence: He had a break in his arm after the fall.
contextPassage: A break can happen when a bone in your body cracks or splits. In sports, a bad fall can sometimes cause a break in your arm or leg.

definition: The first meal you eat in the morning.
exampleSentence: The athlete eats a big breakfast before the game.
contextPassage: Breakfast is the first meal of the day, and it is very important. It gives your body and mind the energy they need to start the day well.

definition: The shape and size of a person's body.
exampleSentence: The athlete has a strong build from training every day.
contextPassage: A person's build tells us if they are tall, short, big, or small. Engineers and sports trainers look at a person's build to understand their body better.

definition: A structure with walls and a roof where people live or work.
exampleSentence: The engineers made a very tall building in the city.
contextPassage: A building is a structure that people make with walls, floors, and a roof. Engineers plan and design buildings to make sure they are safe and strong.

definition: A place or group where people work together to sell things or help others.
exampleSentence: She started a small business to sell food near the school.
contextPassage: A business is a place where people work to make and sell things. Learning about business helps you understand how money and work come together.

definition: A soft, yellow food made from milk. People spread it on bread.
exampleSentence: She puts butter on her toast every morning.
contextPassage: Butter is a common food that people eat every day. It has a lot of fat, so eating too much butter is not good for your health.

definition: A small flying insect that makes honey and lives in a group.
exampleSentence: A by landed on the flower in the garden.
contextPassage: A by collects pollen from flowers. It is a very important insect because it helps plants grow.

definition: To talk to someone on the phone, or to say someone's name out loud.
exampleSentence: Please call the doctor if you feel very sick.
contextPassage: In health and law, people often need to call for help quickly. You can call a doctor or a lawyer when you need important help.

definition: A tool that takes photos or videos.
exampleSentence: The engineer used a camera to take photos of the broken bridge.
contextPassage: A camera is a very useful tool in engineering. Engineers use cameras to look at buildings and machines and find problems.

definition: A word that means something is not possible or not allowed.
exampleSentence: You cannot park your car here because it is not allowed.
contextPassage: In law and education, the word cannot tells people what is not allowed. For example, a student cannot copy another student's work.

definition: Things like buildings, tools, and machines that people use to make or do work.
exampleSentence: The school used its capital to buy new computers for the students.
contextPassage: Capital is very important in law and education. It includes things like buildings and tools that help people do their work every day.

definition: A vehicle with four wheels that people drive to go from one place to another.
exampleSentence: The engineer checked the car's engine to find the problem.
contextPassage: A car is a very common way to travel. Engineers design and fix cars to make sure they are safe and work well.

definition: The type of work a person does for most of their life.
exampleSentence: She has a long career as a doctor and helps many people.
contextPassage: A career is the work you choose to do for many years. People can have careers in many areas, like sports, law, health, or education.

definition: A long, orange vegetable that grows in the ground. It is good to eat.
exampleSentence: He eats a carrot every day because it is good for his eyes.
contextPassage: A carrot is a healthy vegetable that is good for your body. Doctors say eating carrots helps keep your eyes and body strong.

definition: To hold something and move it from one place to another.
exampleSentence: The player had to carry the ball across the field to score.
contextPassage: In sports and engineering, carry means to move something from one place to another. For example, a worker can carry heavy tools to a work site.

definition: The middle of something, or a place where people go for a specific activity.
exampleSentence: She goes to the health centre near her home every week.
contextPassage: A centre is an important place where people go to get help or learn. For example, a health centre is a place where doctors help sick people.

definition: A period of one hundred years.
exampleSentence: People used horses to travel in the nineteenth century.
contextPassage: A century is one hundred years. We use this word to talk about history and to say when important things happened a long time ago.

definition: A piece of furniture to sit on, or the person who leads a meeting.
exampleSentence: The chair of the meeting asked everyone to sit down and listen.
contextPassage: In law and education, a chair is the person who leads a group or meeting. This person helps everyone talk and make decisions together.

definition: When something becomes different from what it was before.
exampleSentence: The doctor said she needs to change what she eats to be healthy.
contextPassage: Change happens in many parts of life. In health, sports, and education, change can help people get better and learn new things.

definition: A picture or drawing that shows information in a simple and clear way.
exampleSentence: The doctor looked at a chart to see if the patient was getting better.
contextPassage: A chart is a very useful tool for showing information. Doctors, engineers, and teachers use charts to understand numbers and facts quickly.

definition: In chess, when the king is in danger and must move.
exampleSentence: He moved his queen and said 'check' to the other player.
contextPassage: In chess, 'check' means the king is in danger. The player must move the king to a safe place.

definition: A food made from milk. It is soft or hard and people eat it every day.
exampleSentence: She puts cheese on her sandwich every morning.
contextPassage: Cheese is a food made from milk. It has protein and calcium, and these are good for your body.

definition: A common farm bird. People eat its meat and eggs.
exampleSentence: She eats chicken for lunch because it is healthy.
contextPassage: Chicken is a bird that lives on farms. Its meat is a healthy food with a lot of protein.

definition: A young person, not yet an adult.
exampleSentence: The child drinks milk every day to stay healthy.
contextPassage: A child is a young person who is still growing. Doctors, teachers, and laws all work to keep children safe and healthy.

definition: To pick one thing from two or more things.
exampleSentence: You can choose to eat fruit or vegetables for a snack.
contextPassage: When you choose, you pick the thing you want. It is important to choose healthy food and good habits every day.

definition: A very big town where many people live and work.
exampleSentence: She lives in a big city with many hospitals and schools.
contextPassage: A city is a large place where many people live. Cities have hospitals, schools, and laws to help the people who live there.

definition: A group of people or things that are the same in some way.
exampleSentence: All students in this class are eight years old.
contextPassage: A class is a group of people or things that are alike. In school, a class is a group of students who learn together.

definition: A room in a school where students learn.
exampleSentence: The students sit in the classroom and listen to the teacher.
contextPassage: A classroom is a room where students go to learn. The teacher talks and students read and write in the classroom.

definition: Free from dirt. Not dirty.
exampleSentence: She washes her hands to keep them clean.
contextPassage: When something is clean, it has no dirt on it. It is important to keep your body and your home clean to stay healthy.

definition: To go up something, like a hill or a wall.
exampleSentence: He likes to climb a big hill every weekend.
contextPassage: When you climb, you move your body up. Climbing is good exercise and it makes your legs and arms strong.

definition: A machine on a wall or table that shows the time.
exampleSentence: The clock on the wall says it is three o'clock.
contextPassage: A clock is a tool that shows the time. In sports and in everyday life, we look at a clock to know what time it is.

definition: To shut something, like a door or a book.
exampleSentence: Please close the door when you leave the room.
contextPassage: When you close something, you shut it. In law and in school, you often hear words like 'close the case' or 'close your book.'

definition: The things you wear on your body, like a shirt or pants.
exampleSentence: He wears warm clothes when he plays sports outside.
contextPassage: Clothes are the things we put on our body every day. When you play sports, it is important to wear the right clothes to move well.

definition: A group of people who do the same activity together.
exampleSentence: She is in a swimming club at her school.
contextPassage: A club is a group of people who meet to do something they like. You can join a sports club or a school club to learn new things and meet new friends.

definition: Not warm. Having a low temperature.
exampleSentence: It is cold outside, so he wears a warm jacket.
contextPassage: When something is cold, it has a low temperature. In sports and in health, cold weather can affect how your body feels.

definition: A school where people study after high school to learn more and get a job.
exampleSentence: She goes to college to study engineering.
contextPassage: College is a place where people learn new skills for work. Many people go to college to become doctors, lawyers, or engineers.

definition: What you see when light hits something, like red, blue, or green.
exampleSentence: The teacher asks the students what colour the sky is.
contextPassage: Light gives everything a colour. When we see red or blue, it is because of how light works.

definition: Something that many people have or use; not rare.
exampleSentence: A cold is a very common illness.
contextPassage: When something is common, many people know it or see it often. For example, a cold is a common illness because many people get it.

definition: A group of people who work together to do a job or sell something.
exampleSentence: She works for a big company that builds bridges.
contextPassage: A company is a group of people who work together. They work to make things or help people.

definition: To look at two or more things and see how they are the same or different.
exampleSentence: The doctor wants to compare the two test results.
contextPassage: When we compare things, we look at them together. We want to see what is the same and what is different.

definition: To finish something fully, with nothing missing.
exampleSentence: Please complete the form before your visit to the doctor.
contextPassage: When you complete something, you finish all parts of it. For example, you complete a test by answering all the questions.

definition: A machine that stores information and helps people do many tasks.
exampleSentence: The engineer uses a computer to draw the new building.
contextPassage: A computer is a very useful machine. People use it to work, learn, and find information.

definition: When two or more people talk to each other.
exampleSentence: The doctor has a short conversation with the patient.
contextPassage: A conversation is when people talk and listen to each other. It helps people share ideas and understand each other.

definition: To make food hot and ready to eat.
exampleSentence: She likes to cook fresh vegetables for dinner.
contextPassage: When you cook food, you make it safe and good to eat. Cooking healthy food is good for your body.

definition: The act of making food ready to eat by using heat.
exampleSentence: Cooking vegetables at home is good for your health.
contextPassage: Cooking is how we prepare food to eat. When we cook at home, we can choose healthy foods for our body.

definition: Right; without any mistakes.
exampleSentence: The student gives the correct answer to the math question.
contextPassage: When something is correct, it has no mistakes. In school and work, it is important to give correct answers.

definition: The amount of money you pay for something.
exampleSentence: The cost of the medicine is ten dollars.
contextPassage: The cost of something tells you how much money you need to buy it. Some things cost a lot of money and some cost a little.

definition: A word that shows someone is able to do something, often in the past.
exampleSentence: The student could answer all the questions on the test.
contextPassage: We use the word could to talk about something a person was able to do. For example, she could read when she was five years old.

definition: A large area of land with its own people and government, like France or Japan.
exampleSentence: Every country has its own laws.
contextPassage: A country is a big place where many people live together. Each country has its own rules and ways of doing things.

definition: A set of lessons about one subject; or the order in which things happen.
exampleSentence: He takes a course in computer science at his college.
contextPassage: A course is a group of lessons that teach you about one topic. You can take a course at school or online to learn something new.

definition: The thick, white part of milk. It floats to the top of milk.
exampleSentence: The doctor said to put cream on the skin to make it feel better.
contextPassage: Cream comes from milk. It has a lot of fat, so doctors and health experts talk about how much cream is good or bad to eat.

definition: To make something new that did not exist before.
exampleSentence: The teacher wants to create a fun new game for the students.
contextPassage: People create things every day. A doctor can create a health plan, and a teacher can create a lesson for students.

definition: The way of life of a group of people. It includes their food, music, habits, and beliefs.
exampleSentence: The school helps students learn about culture from other countries.
contextPassage: Every group of people has its own culture. In school, we learn about different cultures to understand people better.

definition: A small container with a handle. You drink from it.
exampleSentence: The player drank water from a cup after the game.
contextPassage: In sports, players drink water or juice from a cup to stay healthy. A cup is easy to hold and use.

definition: A person who buys something from a shop or business.
exampleSentence: The customer asked the doctor for help at the health clinic.
contextPassage: A customer is a person who gets a service or product. In a health clinic, a customer is a person who comes to see a doctor.

definition: When you use something sharp to divide or open something.
exampleSentence: The player got a small cut on his arm during the game.
contextPassage: A cut can happen when something sharp touches the skin. In sports, small cuts can happen, and it is important to clean them fast.

definition: A number you can divide by two. For example, 2, 4, and 6 are even numbers.
exampleSentence: The teacher asked the students to circle all the even numbers on the page.
contextPassage: Even numbers can be split into two equal groups. In school, we learn that 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 are all even numbers.

definition: Something that happens. It can be big or small.
exampleSentence: The sports event at school was very fun.
contextPassage: An event is something that takes place at a time and a place. A sports event, a school event, or a health event can bring many people together.

definition: At any time. Even one time.
exampleSentence: Have you ever read a book about science?
contextPassage: We use the word ever to ask about any time in the past. In school, teachers ask questions like have you ever studied this before.

definition: All of the people or things in a group. Not one is left out.
exampleSentence: Every student in the class needs to wash their hands.
contextPassage: We use every to talk about all people or things together. A health rule like wash your hands every day is good for all people.

definition: All people. Every single person.
exampleSentence: The teacher said everybody must read the book.
contextPassage: Everybody means all the people in a place or group. In school, when a teacher says everybody, it means no one is left out.

definition: Every person. All people together.
exampleSentence: The law says everyone must follow the rules.
contextPassage: Everyone means all people, not just some. In law and in school, rules are made for everyone so that things are fair.

definition: All things. Not one thing is left out.
exampleSentence: The student checked everything before the exam.
contextPassage: Everything means all the things we are talking about. In school, a good student reads everything and checks everything before a test.

definition: A test or check. It shows what you know or how healthy you are.
exampleSentence: The student studied hard for the big exam.
contextPassage: An exam can be a school test or a health check by a doctor. Both types of exams help us see what we know or how we feel.

definition: One thing that shows what all things in a group are like.
exampleSentence: The teacher showed one example of how to do the math problem.
contextPassage: An example helps us understand something better. In school, a teacher uses an example to show students how to do something new.

definition: To have strong happy feelings about something.
exampleSentence: The fans are excited about the big game today.
contextPassage: In sports, players and fans can feel excited before a match. This strong feeling gives people energy and makes them happy.

definition: Something that makes you feel very happy and full of energy.
exampleSentence: The football match was very exciting to watch.
contextPassage: In sports and school, exciting things make people want to pay attention. When something is exciting, it is fun and interesting.

definition: An activity you do to practice a skill or to keep your body strong and healthy.
exampleSentence: The teacher gave the students a writing exercise to do in class.
contextPassage: Exercise can mean moving your body, like running or swimming, to stay healthy. It can also mean a school activity that helps you practice and learn something new.

definition: Something that costs a lot of money.
exampleSentence: The new school books are very expensive.
contextPassage: Some things in life, like medicine or school tools, can be very expensive. When something is expensive, not everyone can buy it easily.

definition: To say or show something clearly so other people can understand it.
exampleSentence: The doctor will explain how to take the medicine.
contextPassage: When you explain something, you help other people understand it better. Teachers, doctors, and lawyers often explain things so people know what to do.

definition: Something more than what is normal or needed.
exampleSentence: The coach gave the players extra time to practice.
contextPassage: In sports and school, extra means something added on top of what you already have. For example, extra practice or extra homework helps you get better at something.

definition: The part of your body that you use to see things.
exampleSentence: The doctor checked my eye to make sure it was healthy.
contextPassage: Your eyes are very important parts of your body. They help you see the world around you, so it is important to take good care of them.

definition: The front part of your head, with your eyes, nose, and mouth.
exampleSentence: The doctor looked at the child's face to check for any problems.
contextPassage: Your face is the front part of your head. Doctors look at a person's face to check if they are healthy or sick.

definition: Something that is true and real, not made up.
exampleSentence: It is a fact that exercise is good for your health.
contextPassage: A fact is something that is really true. In school, science, and law, facts are very important because they help us know what is real.

definition: When something or someone moves down to the ground.
exampleSentence: The player had a bad fall during the game.
contextPassage: In sports, a fall can happen when a player trips or loses balance. A fall can hurt the body, so it is important to be careful when playing sports.

definition: Something that is not true or not correct.
exampleSentence: The student wrote a false answer on the test.
contextPassage: In school and law, false means something is not true. It is important to know what is true and what is false so we can make good decisions.

definition: A group of people who are closely related, like a mother, father, and children.
exampleSentence: The law helps protect every family.
contextPassage: A family is a group of people who love and care for each other. Families are important in health and in law because they help and support each other.

definition: Someone or something that many people know about.
exampleSentence: She is a famous tennis player.
contextPassage: In sports, some players become famous because they are very good at what they do. Many people know their names and want to watch them play.

definition: Moving or traveling at a high speed, with few stops.
exampleSentence: We took the fast train to get to the game quickly.
contextPassage: A fast train does not stop at every station, so it gets to the destination quickly. People take fast trains when they want to travel a long way in a short time.

definition: A substance in food and in the body that gives energy but can be unhealthy in large amounts.
exampleSentence: The doctor said eating too much fat is not good for you.
contextPassage: Fat is something found in many foods we eat. A little fat is normal and gives our body energy, but too much fat can be bad for our health.

definition: A man who has a child.
exampleSentence: The father signed the paper for his child.
contextPassage: In law, a father is a man who has a child. The law uses this word to talk about who is responsible for a child.

definition: To notice something in your body, like pain or cold.
exampleSentence: I feel pain in my arm today.
contextPassage: In health, we use 'feel' to talk about how the body is. A doctor asks 'How do you feel?' to learn about your symptoms.

definition: To touch something and notice what it is like.
exampleSentence: She likes the feeling of soft cloth on her hand.
contextPassage: 'Feeling' means using your hands to touch and learn about something. In school, children learn about the world by touching and feeling different things.

definition: A small number of things or people.
exampleSentence: A few students are in the classroom today.
contextPassage: We use 'few' to talk about a small number of things. In school, a teacher may say 'We have a few minutes left' to mean not a lot of time.

definition: The number 15.
exampleSentence: There are fifteen players on the field.
contextPassage: Fifteen is the number after fourteen. In sports and school, we use this number to count players, points, or students.

definition: Number five in order; coming after the fourth.
exampleSentence: She finished in fifth place in the race.
contextPassage: We use 'fifth' to show the order of something. In law, sports, and school, it helps us know the position of a person or thing in a list.

definition: The number 50.
exampleSentence: There are fifty questions on the test.
contextPassage: Fifty is the number after forty-nine. In school and everyday life, we use this number to count things like questions, people, or items.

definition: To put something into a space until it is full.
exampleSentence: Please fill in your name on the form.
contextPassage: In law and health, people often fill in forms with their information. In engineering, 'fill' can also mean to put material into a space.

definition: A very thin layer of something on a surface.
exampleSentence: There is a film of dust on the table.
contextPassage: A film is a very thin covering on top of something. In science and health, a film can cover a surface and sometimes make it hard to see through.

definition: The last one; coming at the end.
exampleSentence: She passed her final exam at school.
contextPassage: 'Final' means something is last and there is nothing after it. In law, a final decision cannot be changed, and in sports, the final is the last and most important game.

definition: Something valuable that someone discovers.
exampleSentence: The old coin was a great find at the dig site.
contextPassage: A find is something special that a person discovers. In education, a find can be an old object or a very talented person that someone discovers.

definition: Money you must pay as a punishment for doing something wrong.
exampleSentence: He got a fine for parking in the wrong place.
contextPassage: In law, a fine is money a person must pay when they break a rule. It is a common way to punish people who do something wrong.

definition: The end of something.
exampleSentence: The runner was happy at the finish of the race.
contextPassage: 'Finish' means the end of something, like a race or a job. In sports, athletes try to get to the finish first, and in engineering, a finish can mean the final look of a surface.

definition: The hot, bright flames you see when something burns.
exampleSentence: The fire in the building was very dangerous.
contextPassage: Fire is very hot and can be very dangerous. In engineering and health, it is important to know about fire safety to keep people safe.

definition: The one that comes before all others.
exampleSentence: She was the first student to finish the test.
contextPassage: 'First' means number one in order. In sports, law, and school, we use 'first' to show who or what is at the beginning or in the top position.

definition: An animal that lives in water. People eat fish to stay healthy.
exampleSentence: I eat fish two times a week because it is good for my body.

exampleSentence: There is a short space of time between the two classes.
contextPassage: In school, a space of time means a break or gap between activities. Teachers use this word to talk about how much time students have to finish their work.

definition: To say words out loud to someone.
exampleSentence: The student stands up to speak in front of the class.
contextPassage: In school, it is important to speak clearly so others can understand you. In law, people speak in court to share what they know.

definition: A lower price for something, only for a short time.
exampleSentence: The hospital has a special on health check-ups this week.
contextPassage: A special means you can buy something for less money than usual. It does not last a long time, so you need to act fast.

definition: To write or say the letters of a word in the correct order.
exampleSentence: Can you spell your name for me?
contextPassage: In school, students learn to spell new words every week. Knowing how to spell helps you write clearly and correctly.

definition: The correct order of letters in a word.
exampleSentence: The teacher checks our spelling in every writing test.
contextPassage: Good spelling helps people understand what you write. In school, students practice spelling so they can write words the right way.

definition: A physical activity or game that people do for fun or to stay healthy.
exampleSentence: My favourite sport is football.
contextPassage: Playing a sport is a great way to keep your body strong and healthy. Schools often have sport classes so students can be active every day.

definition: To hurt a part of your body, like your ankle, by moving it the wrong way.
exampleSentence: He fell and spring his ankle during the game.
contextPassage: When you spring a joint, it means the muscles or ligaments around it are hurt. This can happen when you move too fast or in the wrong direction.

definition: To be on your feet in an upright position.
exampleSentence: The players stand on the field before the game begins.
contextPassage: In sports, players often stand in a line before a match starts. Standing can also mean to stay strong, like a team that does not give up.

definition: The beginning of something.
exampleSentence: The race has a fast start today.
contextPassage: Every activity has a start, which is the moment it begins. In school and sports, a good start helps you do well from the beginning.

definition: A round, red fruit that people often eat in salads and cooking.
exampleSentence: I put a tomato in my salad for lunch.
contextPassage: A tomato is very good for your health because it has vitamins and water inside. People eat tomatoes in many ways, like in soups, salads, and sauces.

definition: A hard, white part inside your mouth that you use to bite and chew food.
exampleSentence: She brushes every tooth before she goes to bed.
contextPassage: Your teeth help you eat and speak every day. It is important to keep your teeth clean and healthy by brushing them two times a day.

definition: The main subject or idea that people talk or write about.
exampleSentence: The topic of today's lesson is animals.
contextPassage: Every lesson in school has a topic, which is the main thing you will learn about. Knowing the topic helps students focus and understand the lesson better.

definition: Cars, buses, and people moving on roads.
exampleSentence: There is a lot of traffic on the road in the morning.
contextPassage: Traffic means all the vehicles and people moving on streets and roads. Engineers work to make roads safe so traffic can move well and without problems.

definition: A long vehicle that moves on rails and carries people or things.
exampleSentence: We take the train to get to school every morning.
contextPassage: A train runs on metal tracks and can carry many people at one time. Engineers build train systems to help people travel quickly and easily between places.

definition: Something that is correct and matches the facts.
exampleSentence: It is true that students must do their homework.
contextPassage: In school, a true answer is one that is correct and right. In law, people must say only true things so that everyone can trust what they hear.

definition: To make an effort to do something.
exampleSentence: She tries to run faster every day at practice.
contextPassage: In sports, you try your best to win. In school, you try hard to learn new things.

definition: A group of things that are the same in some way.
exampleSentence: This type of bridge is very strong.
contextPassage: In engineering, there are many types of machines. Each type does a different job.

definition: Below something, or controlled by something.
exampleSentence: The pipes are under the floor.
contextPassage: In engineering, many parts are under the ground. In law, people must act under the rules of the country.

definition: To know what something means.
exampleSentence: The student wants to understand the new law.
contextPassage: In school, it is important to understand what you read. In law, people need to understand the rules so they can follow them.

definition: A big school where people go to learn after high school.
exampleSentence: She goes to university to study science.
contextPassage: A university is a place where people learn many subjects. Students go there to get a job one day.

definition: Up to a time when something happens.
exampleSentence: You must wait until the judge speaks.
contextPassage: In law, a person must wait until the court makes a decision. Nothing changes until that time.

definition: To do something with a tool or object to get a result.
exampleSentence: The doctor uses this tool to help sick people.
contextPassage: We use many tools in life every day. In school, at work, and at home, we use things to help us.

definition: Something that helps you or makes a job easier.
exampleSentence: A dictionary is very useful in class.
contextPassage: In school, some tools are very useful for learning. A useful book or app can help you understand new words fast.

definition: A plant that you can eat.
exampleSentence: Carrots are a good vegetable for your body.
contextPassage: Vegetables are plants that are good for your health. Doctors say you should eat vegetables every day.

definition: A moving picture with sound that you watch on a screen.
exampleSentence: The teacher shows a video about animals in class.
contextPassage: Videos can help you learn new things in school. You can watch a video to understand something better.

definition: A trip to see a person or a place.
exampleSentence: She makes a visit to the doctor every year.
contextPassage: A visit to the doctor is important for your health. Students can also make a visit to a new school or museum.

definition: A person who comes to see someone or something.
exampleSentence: A visitor came to the hospital to see his friend.
contextPassage: In a hospital, a visitor is someone who comes to see a sick person. Schools also have visitors who come to help students learn.

definition: A time when you stop and do nothing until something happens.
exampleSentence: There is a long wait to see the doctor today.
contextPassage: At a hospital, there is sometimes a wait before you can see a doctor. The wait can be short or long.

definition: To move on your feet, one step at a time, not fast.
exampleSentence: The doctor says to walk for thirty minutes every day.
contextPassage: Walking is good for your body and your health. In sports, some people walk long distances to stay strong.

definition: A tall, flat structure made of stone or brick that stands up straight.
exampleSentence: The engineers built a strong wall around the building.
contextPassage: A wall can keep people safe. Long ago, people built walls to protect their towns from danger.

definition: A little hot, but in a good and comfortable way.
exampleSentence: You should do warm exercises before you run.
contextPassage: Before sports, it is good to make your body warm. This helps your muscles and stops you from getting hurt.

definition: To clean something with water.
exampleSentence: You should wash your hands before you eat.
contextPassage: Washing your hands is very important for your health. It removes bad things from your skin and stops you from getting sick.

definition: A small clock that you wear on your wrist.
exampleSentence: The runner looked at his watch to see his time.
contextPassage: A watch helps you know how much time you have in a game or race. Many sports players wear a watch to check their speed and time.

definition: A clear liquid that we drink and find in rivers, lakes, and rain.
exampleSentence: Drink water after you run to feel better.
contextPassage: Water is very important for your body and your health. When you do sports, you lose water, so you need to drink more.

definition: The clothes you put on your body for a specific activity.
exampleSentence: You need the right wear to play football safely.
contextPassage: Good sports wear helps you move and play better. It also keeps your body safe when you do sports.

definition: What it is like outside, for example hot, cold, rainy, or sunny.
exampleSentence: The weather is cold today, so the game is inside.
contextPassage: Weather tells us if it is hot, cold, or rainy outside. In sports, bad weather can stop a game or change how people play.

definition: A place on the internet where you can find information and read pages.
exampleSentence: You can go to the school website to find your homework.
contextPassage: A website is like a book on the internet with many pages. You can use a website to learn new things or find important information.

definition: Saturday and Sunday, the two days when most people do not work or go to school.
exampleSentence: We play football together every weekend.
contextPassage: The weekend is a great time to do sports and be active. Many games and sports events happen on Saturday and Sunday.

definition: Feeling good and healthy, not sick.
exampleSentence: She is well now and can go back to school.
contextPassage: When you are well, your body feels strong and good. Eating good food and sleeping enough helps you stay well.

definition: A word you use to ask about one thing from a group of things.
exampleSentence: Which law says we must stop at a red light?
contextPassage: We use the word which to ask about a specific thing from many things. In law, people use which to ask about the right rule or the right answer.

definition: To want or wish for something strongly.
exampleSentence: The people will a fair law for everyone.
contextPassage: In law, the word will can mean what a person wants or wishes. It is important because laws try to respect what people need and want.

definition: To beat the other team or person in a game or sport.
exampleSentence: Our team worked hard to win the game.
contextPassage: To win means you do better than the other team or player. In sports, people train a lot because they want to win.

definition: A clear opening in a wall that lets in light and air.
exampleSentence: The engineer put a big window in the new building.
contextPassage: Windows let light and air come into a building. Engineers think carefully about where to put windows so a building is bright and comfortable.

definition: The coldest time of the year, when days are short and the weather is cold.
exampleSentence: We play indoor sports in winter because it is too cold outside.
contextPassage: Winter is the coldest part of the year. Some sports, like skiing and ice skating, are only possible in winter.

definition: A single unit of language that has a meaning, like cat, run, or big.
exampleSentence: The teacher asked the students to learn one new word every day.
contextPassage: A word is a small piece of language that means something. Learning new words helps you read, write, and talk better.

definition: To do a job or task; the things you do to get something done.
exampleSentence: The engineer works hard to fix the bridge.
contextPassage: Work is something people do every day in many places, like schools, hospitals, and offices. In every job, good work helps people and makes things better.

definition: A person who does a job to earn money.
exampleSentence: The worker builds the new road every day.
contextPassage: A worker is someone who does important jobs for us. Workers help in hospitals, schools, and buildings to make life safe and good.

definition: All the countries and people on Earth.
exampleSentence: Doctors help sick people all over the world.
contextPassage: The world means every place and every person on Earth. We learn about the world in school so we can understand people in other countries.

definition: A word we use to talk about something that could happen in a different situation.
exampleSentence: The doctor would help you if you go to the clinic.
contextPassage: We use 'would' to talk about things that are possible but not happening right now. For example, a teacher would explain a lesson again if you do not understand.

definition: To make words or letters on paper or a screen.
exampleSentence: The lawyer will write the new rules on paper.
contextPassage: Writing is a very important skill in school and at work. Lawyers write rules, doctors write notes, and students write answers to learn new things.

definition: A person who writes words, books, or documents.
exampleSentence: The writer made a book about the school rules.
contextPassage: A writer is someone who puts words together to share ideas or information. In law and education, writers help people understand important rules and lessons.

definition: Words that someone puts on paper or a screen.
exampleSentence: Good writing helps the doctor understand the patient.
contextPassage: Writing is a key skill in many jobs. Lawyers use writing to make rules, and students use writing to show what they know in school.

definition: Something that is not correct or not good.
exampleSentence: It is wrong to hit another player in a game.
contextPassage: When something is wrong, it can hurt people or cause problems. In school and sports, we learn the difference between right and wrong to be fair and kind.

definition: A period of 12 months or 365 days.
exampleSentence: The young player trains hard every year.
contextPassage: A year has 12 months, and we use it to count time in many ways. Doctors, teachers, and engineers all plan their work by the year.

definition: A bright color like the sun or a lemon.
exampleSentence: The referee showed the player a yellow card.
contextPassage: Yellow is a very bright and easy-to-see color. In sports, a yellow card is a warning to a player who does something wrong in the game.

definition: A word we say to agree or to say something is true.
exampleSentence: The student said yes to answer the teacher's question.
contextPassage: We say 'yes' to agree with someone or to say something is correct. In school and in law, saying yes or no clearly is very important.

definition: The day before today.
exampleSentence: The teacher talked about the law yesterday in class.
contextPassage: Yesterday means the day that came before today. In school and in law, people often talk about what happened yesterday to understand events and facts.

definition: The person or people someone is talking to.
exampleSentence: The doctor says you need to drink more water.
contextPassage: We use 'you' to talk directly to another person. Teachers, doctors, and lawyers use 'you' to give help and information to the right person.

definition: Not old; a person or thing that has lived for a short time.
exampleSentence: The young player runs fast on the field.
contextPassage: Young people are not old, and they are still learning and growing. In schools and sports, young people get help to build good habits for a healthy life.

definition: A word that shows something belongs to the person you are talking to.
exampleSentence: The nurse asks, 'What is your name?'
contextPassage: We use 'your' to talk about things that belong to the person we are speaking to. Doctors and teachers use 'your' to ask about you and help you better.

definition: You, and nobody else. It talks about the same person as 'you'.
exampleSentence: You need to take care of yourself every day.
contextPassage: In health, sports, and school, 'yourself' is a very important word. It helps you talk about things you do for you, like feeding yourself, moving your body, or teaching yourself new things.
